title: Exterior House Painting

budget.description: Single-story home under 1,500 sq ft, basic acrylic latex paint, one coat over existing color, minimal prep

midRange.description: Two-story home 1,500-2,500 sq ft, quality acrylic latex, two coats, primer on bare spots, full prep and caulking

premium.description: Large or multi-story home, premium paint, complete scraping and sanding, primer coat, two finish coats, trim and accent colors

costFactors.title: Home Size and Stories
costFactors.description: A 1,500 sq ft single-story home has roughly 1,000-1,200 sq ft of paintable exterior. A 2,500 sq ft two-story home has 1,800-2,500 sq ft of paintable surface. Two-story and three-story homes cost more per square foot because of scaffolding, ladder work, and slower production. Expect to pay 20-40% more per square foot for each additional story.

costFactors.title: Paint Quality
costFactors.description: Builder-grade exterior paint costs $20-$30 per gallon. Mid-range options like Behr Premium or Sherwin-Williams SuperPaint run $35-$55 per gallon. Premium lines like Duration, Emerald, or Benjamin Moore Aura Exterior cost $60-$85 per gallon. A typical home uses 10-20 gallons. Better paint lasts 10-15 years versus 5-7 for budget paint.

costFactors.title: Prep Work Required
costFactors.description: Light prep (power washing, minor scraping) adds little to the cost. Extensive peeling, cracking, or chalking requires heavy scraping, sanding, and spot priming - adding $500-$2,000 in labor. Lead paint abatement on pre-1978 homes adds $1,000-$3,000 or more and requires certified contractors.

costFactors.title: Trim, Doors, and Accent Colors
costFactors.description: Many quotes cover body paint only. Painting trim, fascia, soffits, shutters, doors, and window frames adds time and cost. Trim painting typically adds $300-$800. Multiple accent colors add $200-$500 because of extra masking and brush work. Ask whether trim is included in every quote you get.

costFactors.title: Siding Material
costFactors.description: Smooth surfaces like vinyl or aluminum siding paint quickly. Wood clapboard and shingles absorb more paint and require more prep. Stucco and brick are porous and may need special primers and extra coats. Textured surfaces can use 20-30% more paint than smooth ones, adding $200-$600 in material costs alone.

materialOptions.pros: Easy cleanup, low odor, flexible in temperature changes, good durability
materialOptions.cons: Less durable than premium options, may need repainting in 5-7 years
materialOptions.bestFor: Most homes, budget to mid-range projects

materialOptions.pros: Superior adhesion, fade resistance, 10-15 year lifespan, better coverage
materialOptions.bestFor: Long-term homes where you want 10+ years between paint jobs

materialOptions.pros: Bridges hairline cracks, waterproof, excellent for stucco and masonry
materialOptions.cons: Thick application, not ideal for wood siding, harder to apply
materialOptions.bestFor: Stucco, concrete block, and masonry exteriors with minor cracking

materialOptions.pros: Extremely hard finish, excellent adhesion to bare wood, self-leveling
materialOptions.cons: Strong fumes, slower drying, harder cleanup, restricted in some states
materialOptions.bestFor: Bare wood trim, areas needing maximum adhesion, repainting over old oil paint

materialOptions.pros: Shows natural wood grain, penetrates into wood, easier maintenance recoats
materialOptions.cons: Less protection than paint, shorter lifespan (3-5 years), limited colors
materialOptions.bestFor: Cedar, redwood, and other natural wood siding where grain is desired

northeast.label: Northeast
northeast.note: Shorter painting season drives higher labor rates

westCoast.label: West Coast
westCoast.note: Highest labor rates, environmental regulations on paint types

southeast.label: Southeast
southeast.note: Year-round painting weather, lower labor costs

midwest.label: Midwest
midwest.note: Lower labor rates, shorter painting season in northern areas

mountainWest.label: Mountain West
mountainWest.note: Dry climate is good for paint longevity, growing labor market

diyVsPro.diyFeasibility: Moderate for single-story homes; not recommended for multi-story
diyVsPro.diySavings: 50-65% savings on labor for single-story homes
diyTasks: Caulking gaps around windows and doors
Painting first-floor walls with a roller and brush
Painting low trim and fascia boards
proOnlyTasks: Multi-story work requiring scaffolding or tall ladders
Lead paint testing and abatement on pre-1978 homes
High gable ends and peak areas
Significant wood repair or rot replacement before painting
diyVsPro.diyRisks: Falls are the biggest danger - ladder accidents send over 100,000 people to the ER annually. Multi-story homes require scaffolding that costs $500-$1,500 to rent. Poor prep leads to peeling within 1-2 years. A DIY exterior paint job takes most homeowners 3-5 weekends versus 4-6 days for a crew.

savingsTips: Paint in the off-season (late fall or early spring) when painters are less busy and may discount 10-20%
Do the prep work yourself (washing, scraping, caulking) and hire pros just for the painting - saves $500-$1,500
Stick to one body color with minimal trim contrast to reduce masking time and multiple paint purchases
Buy paint during manufacturer rebate periods - Sherwin-Williams and Benjamin Moore run 30-40% off sales several times per year
Invest in premium paint - spending an extra $200-$400 on better paint can add 5-7 years before the next repaint
Skip the primer if existing paint is in good condition and you are staying within the same color family
Get quotes from at least 3-4 painters - exterior painting quotes vary widely, sometimes by 50% or more
Bundle exterior painting with other exterior work (gutters, trim repair) for a package discount

contractorQuestions.question: What prep work is included in your quote?
contractorQuestions.why: Prep is where quality painters earn their money. A quote that includes thorough scraping, sanding, caulking, and priming will last years longer than one that skips prep to save time.

contractorQuestions.question: What paint brand and product line will you use?
contractorQuestions.why: There is a significant difference between a $25 gallon and a $65 gallon of paint. Make sure you know exactly what product is going on your home and look up its expected lifespan.

contractorQuestions.question: How many coats of paint are included?
contractorQuestions.why: Two coats of body paint is the standard for a quality job. One coat is rarely enough for a color change and will show thin spots within a year or two.

contractorQuestions.question: Does the quote include all trim, fascia, soffits, and doors?
contractorQuestions.why: Many quotes only cover the body/siding. Trim, fascia, window frames, and doors are often extra. Clarify exactly what surfaces are included.

contractorQuestions.question: Do you carry liability insurance and workers compensation?
contractorQuestions.why: Exterior painting involves ladders and heights. If an uninsured painter falls off your roof, you could be liable. Always verify active insurance certificates.

contractorQuestions.question: What is your warranty on labor?
contractorQuestions.why: Reputable painters offer a 2-5 year labor warranty covering peeling, blistering, or adhesion failure. Paint manufacturers often offer separate material warranties of 15-25 years.

contractorQuestions.question: Will you test for lead paint on my pre-1978 home?
contractorQuestions.why: Lead paint disturbance requires EPA-certified RRP contractors. Improper removal creates serious health hazards and can result in fines up to $37,500 per day.

secondaryKeywords: cost to paint exterior of house 2026
exterior painting cost per square foot
how much to paint a house exterior
seo.metaDescription: Exterior house painting costs $1,800-$7,500 in 2026. See pricing by home size, paint quality, and prep work with our interactive cost calculator and money-saving tips.
